perturbation.explanation: Reducing the minimum notice period to 1 week could violate UK employment law, which mandates longer notice periods based on the length of service.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 86 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 stipulates statutory minimum notice periods for employees, which increase with service. One week's notice for an employee who has been employed for a significant period (as this agreement suggests by referencing continuous employment from 2013) would likely be a violation of this Act, as it provides for at least one week's notice for each year of employment, up to a maximum of 12 weeks.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the minimum notice period from 6 weeks to 1 week. The Law Explanation states that Section 86 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 mandates notice periods that increase with service, potentially up to 12 weeks, making a 1-week notice period for a long-term employee a violation. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this by stating that reducing the notice period could violate UK employment law, which mandates longer notice periods based on length of service. Snippet 1 is a dead link, and Snippet 2 is empty. However, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, coupled with the change in the original and modified text, indicate a clear contradiction of employment law.

perturbation.original_text: 17.1 The Company may, in its sole and absolute discretion, terminate the Executive's employment under this Agreement at any time and with immediate effect by notifying the Executive that the Company is exercising its right under this clause 17 and that it will make a payment in l ieu of not ice ("PILON") to the Executive. The Executive's employment will terminate immediately and any PILON due to the Executive in accordance with the 8 provisions of this clause 17 will be paid within 28 days. The PILON will be equal to the basic salary (as at the Termination Date) which the Executive would have been entitled to receive under this Agreement during the notice period referred to at clause 2 (or, if notice has already been given, during the remainder of the notice period) less deductions required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 17.1 The Company may, in its sole and absolute discretion, terminate the Executive's employment under this Agreement at any time and with immediate effect by notifying the Executive that the Company is exercising its right under this clause 17 and that it will make a payment in lieu of notice ("PILON") to the Executive. The Executive's employment will terminate immediately and any PILON due to the Executive in accordance with the 8 provisions of this clause 17 will be paid within 90 days. The PILON will be equal to the basic salary (as at the Termination Date) which the Executive would have been entitled to receive under this Agreement during the notice period referred to at clause 2 (or, if notice has already been given, during the remainder of the notice period) less deductions required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the payment of PILON (Payment In Lieu Of Notice) to 90 days could potentially conflict with UK employment law regarding timely payment of wages upon termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 87 doesn't explicitly state a payment deadline, employment law principles imply wages (including PILON) should be paid reasonably promptly after termination. A 90-day delay may be deemed unreasonable, especially if it causes undue hardship to the employee. Other laws governing payments of wages would likely be interpreted to mean at the time the employee would have received them had they worked their notice period
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Understanding your pay: Deductions from your pay - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Understanding your pay Skip contents Deductions from your pay Your employer is not allowed to make deductions unless: it’s required or allowed by law, for example National Insurance, income tax or student loan repayments you agree in writing your contract says they can there’s a statutory payment due to a public authority you have not worked due to taking part in a strike or industrial action there’s been an earlier overpayment of wages or expenses the court has told your employer to take debt payments A deduction cannot normally reduce your pay below the National Minimum Wage even if you agree to it, except if the deduction is for: tax or National Insurance something you’ve done and your contract says you’re liable for it, for example a shortfall in your till if you work in a shop repayment of a loan or advance of wages repayment of an accidental overpayment of wages buying shares or share options in the business accommodation provided by your employer your own use, for example union subscriptions or pension contributions If you work in retail - for example shops, restaurants Your employer cannot take more than 10% from your gross pay (pay before tax and National Insurance) each pay period to cover any shortfalls. Example There’s a shortfall of £50 in your till and your employer wants to deduct this from your earnings. You’re paid £250 gross per week. Your employer can take 10% of your gross earnings, which is £25. They must only take £25 one week and then make another deduction from your next pay cheque for £25. If you leave your job, they can take the full amount owed from your final pay. If you have not been paid in full Speak to your employer first to try to sort out the problem informally. If this does not work, you can: go to Acas (Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service) for help and advice contact your nearest Citizens Advice talk to your trade union representative You have the right to go to an Employment Tribunal to get your money. If you leave your job Check your contract to see if your employer is allowed to withhold your pay. Normally you’re entitled to be paid everything you’ve earned up to the point you finish. If you’re forced to resign because your employer refuses to pay you, you may be able to make a constructive dismissal claim in an Employment Tribunal . perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that PILON will be paid within 28 days. The changed contract extends this to 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this delay might conflict with UK employment law regarding timely wage payments upon termination. The Law Explanation supports this by suggesting a 90-day delay may be deemed unreasonable, with the implication that wages (including PILON) should be paid reasonably promptly after termination. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not explicitly state a timeframe for payment of wages after termination, it emphasizes that an employee is "entitled to be paid everything you’ve earned up to the point you finish", suggesting payment should be made promptly and that withholding or delaying payment can give rise to claims. Therefore, delaying the payment from 28 to 90 days creates a contradiction, as it potentially violates employment law principles for timely payment of wages.

perturbation.original_text: 21.  Post Termination Restrictions During the term of employment and for one (1) year thereafter, the Executive will not directly or indirectly, either themselves or through others, encourage or solicit any employee of the Company to leave the Company for any reason. This obligation shall not affect any responsibility the Executive has as an employee of the Company with respect to the bona fide hiring and firing of Company personnel.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. Post Termination Restrictions During the term of employment and for five (5) years thereafter, the Executive will not directly or indirectly, either themselves or through others, encourage or solicit any employee of the Company to leave the Company for any reason. This obligation shall not affect any responsibility the Executive has as an employee of the Company with respect to the bona fide hiring and firing of Company personnel.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under UK law, particularly if it unduly restricts the Executive's future employment opportunities.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: UK common law dictates that restraint of trade clauses must be reasonable in scope, duration, and geographical area to be enforceable. A five-year non-solicitation clause is likely to be challenged as excessive unless the employer can demonstrate a very strong justification based on the employee's seniority, access to confidential information, and the specific nature of the business. The links refer to a UK court case outlining the principles for interpreting restrictive covenants and an overview article that refers to common law principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from one year to five years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under UK law, and the Law Explanation supports this by stating that restraint of trade clauses must be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable. The scraped law snippet defines 'restraint of trade' as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. While the snippet refers to US law (15 U.S.C. § 1), the Law Explanation specifically invokes UK common law, and extending the non-solicitation period to five years likely creates an unreasonable restraint on the Executive's ability to seek future employment, thus creating a legal contradiction.
